observations: Daily-resolution series spanning ~2019-01 to mid-2021, with a high-frequency wiggle (likely weekly) riding on a slow-moving baseline.
Two broad swings in the level: a peak around 2019-03/04 (~125), a trough around 2019-09/10 (~80), a second hump around 2020-03 (~135), a dip around 2020-07/08 (~105).
From roughly 2020-09 onward the baseline ramps steadily upward from ~110 to a peak near 200 by 2021-03/04 — a strong, sustained climb over many points.
The early portion looks oscillatory/cyclic, but the later portion is dominated by the upward ramp rather than a repeating cycle.
Near the forecast origin (mid-2021) the level appears to have plateaued/rolled over slightly, easing down from ~200 to ~180-185.
The amplitude of the short-term wiggle appears roughly similar throughout (no obvious explosion in noise).
No isolated single-point spikes that snap back; movements are smooth multi-point transitions.
visual.pattern_summary: An early oscillatory/cyclic phase that transitions into a strong gradual upward drift, with the seasonal-looking cycle fading and a possible plateau/turn near the forecast origin.
hypotheses: A forecast fit only to the most recent window may extrapolate the late upward ramp indefinitely, overshooting if the level has truly plateaued or is turning down at the origin.
Conversely, anchoring on the very last few points (which dip from the peak) could project a continued decline that contradicts the longer-run rise.
Treating the early oscillation as a stable repeating cycle could mislead, since that cyclic behavior weakens/changes during the drift phase.
Mistaking the slow drift for a fixed level could produce a flat forecast that lags the trend.
The mixture of regimes (cyclic early, drifting late) means a single recent window may not represent the dynamics relevant just past the origin.
uncertainties: Whether the late plateau/downturn near the origin is a genuine turning point or just short-term noise within a continuing rise.
The true period of the high-frequency wiggle (weekly vs other) and whether it is stable.
Whether the broad swings are a real seasonal/annual cycle or just low-frequency drift; only ~2.5 years are shown.
Exact dates, magnitudes, and whether any level change is permanent versus temporary.
What external factors drove the regime change from oscillation to sustained drift.
Behavior beyond the forecast origin is unknown from this image.

proposal.visual_first_rationale: Visually, the chart shows a clear and sustained multi-month upward climb beginning around 2020-09 and persisting through early 2021 — a smooth, continuous ramp over many data points rather than a sudden step jump. The early portion of the series is oscillatory/cyclic, but from roughly 2020-09 onward the baseline steadily rises from ~110 to ~200, a characteristic gradual drift shape with no sharp discontinuity. There are no isolated single-point spikes that snap back (ruling out temporary events), and the transition into the higher level is too gradual and extended to be modeled as a permanent instantaneous step. This visual read is confirmed by the numeric diagnostics: a candidate_drift_interval is explicitly flagged from 2020-05-25 to 2020-12-07 with a slope of +0.225 per step and a total delta of ~44 units. The transient_event_score of 0.94 is high, but candidate_event_blocks is empty and the chart shows smooth multi-point transitions — not spikes that revert — so the high score likely reflects the regime transition rather than true transient events. The permanent_shift_magnitude (~27) is modest relative to the ~90-unit ramp, and the full_history baseline already performs well (MAE ~9.7, WAPE ~5.1%), meaning the full-history context is valuable and should be kept. A clipped ramp regressor over the primary candidate interval directly models the gradual drift mechanism observed in the chart, giving the model a structural handle on the slow climb without discarding the useful early-period history.
proposal.expected_effect: Adding a clipped ramp regressor over the primary candidate drift interval (2020-05-25 to 2020-12-07) should allow the model to explicitly capture the sustained gradual upward drift seen in the late portion of the series while retaining the full-history seasonal and trend information. This should reduce systematic under/over-shoot in the forecast horizon where the ramp dynamics are most influential, improving on the naive full-history baseline's MAE of ~9.7 and WAPE of ~5.1%.
